label: tetlock-est-retention-duty-personal-devices
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**Does Government Code § 34090 (or any other statutory/common-law preservation duty) extend to communications created and stored exclusively on personal devices/accounts, and did the Signal auto-delete settings — operating before any preservation duty arguably attached — violate that duty?**

What would have to be true: § 34090 and the city's retention schedule would need to be construed to apply beyond records in city custody; and a preservation/litigation-hold obligation would need to be shown to have arisen (e.g., reasonable anticipation of the franchise dispute) before the specific messages auto-deleted, given most deletions predated Pacific Refuse's CPRA request and the eventual lawsuit.
text: {"key_considerations":["§ 34090 is a *destruction-authorization* statute (requires legislative-body approval/city-attorney consent to destroy) that presupposes records are in the city's custody and control; its text and mechanics don't obviously extend to files the city never possessed on councilmembers' personal Signal accounts. San Jose v. Superior Court resolved CPRA *disclosure* scope for personal-device writings but did not hold — and left open — whether such writings become subject to a retention *schedule* adopted under §34090; no controlling CA appellate authority has closed that gap.","Timing cuts against a clean violation: the sub-question's own premise is that most messages auto-deleted before Pacific Refuse's CPRA request or the franchise-award dispute arose. If the theory of duty is common-law spoliation, that duty typically requires litigation to be 'reasonably anticipated,' which is hard to establish for routine RFP-stage discussions months before any bid protest — though the fact one councilmember got 'uneasy' and screenshotted ~40 messages could support an argument that at least some participants had contemporaneous awareness of impropriety, arguably moving up the trigger date for those messages/that person.","If instead the theory is that §34090's retention *period* (4 years for franchise correspondence) attaches automatically at a record's creation regardless of anticipated litigation (a purely statutory, non-spoliation theory), the timing defense is weaker — but this reading requires resolving prong one (that §34090 reaches personal-device records at all) in the affirmative first; the two prongs are not independent.","Attribution/agency problem: §34090 and the retention schedule bind the city's records-management practices; there's no indication the city had adopted a BYOD/personal-device or messaging-app policy imposing retention duties on individual officials' personal accounts, and the disappearing-message settings were reportedly configured by a third-party consultant's Signal group, not the city — complicating any finding that the *city* (as opposed to individual members, who face separate exposure) breached a retention duty.","This is a live, unsettled, and increasingly litigated area post-San Jose (agencies nationally are grappling with ephemeral-messaging apps and public-records/retention law); absent a squarely on-point CA case or AG opinion extending §34090 to personal-device ephemeral messaging, a court could go either way, but the structural and temporal obstacles make an affirmative, clean finding of violation less likely than not."],"probability":28,"what_evidence_would_change_this":"A California appellate decision, AG opinion, or CalOMA/Little Hoover Commission guidance squarely holding that §34090 (or a general constitutional/statutory public-records preservation duty independent of litigation anticipation) reaches personal-device communications meeting the San Jose test would raise this significantly. Conversely, authority confirming §34090 is limited to records within the city's actual custody/control, or evidence that the city had no BYOD/messaging policy and that all deletions predated any city-level knowledge of the dispute, would lower it further. Evidence pinpointing exactly when the city (not just one uneasy councilmember) had reason to anticipate a franchise-award challenge would sharpen the timing analysis for a common-law spoliation theory."}

key_considerations: § 34090 is a *destruction-authorization* statute (requires legislative-body approval/city-attorney consent to destroy) that presupposes records are in the city's custody and control; its text and mechanics don't obviously extend to files the city never possessed on councilmembers' personal Signal accounts. San Jose v. Superior Court resolved CPRA *disclosure* scope for personal-device writings but did not hold — and left open — whether such writings become subject to a retention *schedule* adopted under §34090; no controlling CA appellate authority has closed that gap.

Timing cuts against a clean violation: the sub-question's own premise is that most messages auto-deleted before Pacific Refuse's CPRA request or the franchise-award dispute arose. If the theory of duty is common-law spoliation, that duty typically requires litigation to be 'reasonably anticipated,' which is hard to establish for routine RFP-stage discussions months before any bid protest — though the fact one councilmember got 'uneasy' and screenshotted ~40 messages could support an argument that at least some participants had contemporaneous awareness of impropriety, arguably moving up the trigger date for those messages/that person.

If instead the theory is that §34090's retention *period* (4 years for franchise correspondence) attaches automatically at a record's creation regardless of anticipated litigation (a purely statutory, non-spoliation theory), the timing defense is weaker — but this reading requires resolving prong one (that §34090 reaches personal-device records at all) in the affirmative first; the two prongs are not independent.

Attribution/agency problem: §34090 and the retention schedule bind the city's records-management practices; there's no indication the city had adopted a BYOD/personal-device or messaging-app policy imposing retention duties on individual officials' personal accounts, and the disappearing-message settings were reportedly configured by a third-party consultant's Signal group, not the city — complicating any finding that the *city* (as opposed to individual members, who face separate exposure) breached a retention duty.

This is a live, unsettled, and increasingly litigated area post-San Jose (agencies nationally are grappling with ephemeral-messaging apps and public-records/retention law); absent a squarely on-point CA case or AG opinion extending §34090 to personal-device ephemeral messaging, a court could go either way, but the structural and temporal obstacles make an affirmative, clean finding of violation less likely than not.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: A California appellate decision, AG opinion, or CalOMA/Little Hoover Commission guidance squarely holding that §34090 (or a general constitutional/statutory public-records preservation duty independent of litigation anticipation) reaches personal-device communications meeting the San Jose test would raise this significantly. Conversely, authority confirming §34090 is limited to records within the city's actual custody/control, or evidence that the city had no BYOD/messaging policy and that all deletions predated any city-level knowledge of the dispute, would lower it further. Evidence pinpointing exactly when the city (not just one uneasy councilmember) had reason to anticipate a franchise-award challenge would sharpen the timing analysis for a common-law spoliation theory.
